title: Refresh API usage on hook events when stale
description: When any hook fires (status update arrives at daemon), check if API usage data is empty/missing (displays as just a slash). If empty, trigger an API usage refresh. If usage data already has values, do NOT refresh — be conservative with API calls. Currently API usage is fetched once on daemon start (acd-czjv). Hook events are a natural trigger point to fill in missing data without unnecessary polling.
notes:
## Technical Context for Agent

### What 'empty/missing' means in code
- `UsageState::Unavailable` = no data fetched yet (shows '--' in TUI)
- `UsageState::Blocked` = 403 Forbidden, permanent (shows 'Quota: blocked')
- `UsageState::Available(data)` = data successfully fetched

### Trigger condition
Refresh when `matches!(*state, UsageState::Unavailable)`. Do NOT refresh when Blocked (permanent) or Available (already has data).

### Implementation path
- `UsageFetcher::fetch_once()` is currently private (`async fn`)
- Need to either make it `pub(crate)` or add a public `trigger_refresh()` method
- Hook events arrive at daemon via IPC `handle_set_command()` in `src/daemon/handlers/mod.rs`
- The handler has access to `DaemonState` which contains the `UsageFetcher`
- After processing a hook event, check usage state and trigger refresh if Unavailable

### 403 interaction
The `fetch_once()` method already checks the `blocked` flag internally and returns early if set. So even if called when Blocked, it won't make network requests. But the trigger check should still prefer checking state first to avoid unnecessary async calls.
close_reason: Refresh API usage on hook events when UsageState::Unavailable. Added trigger_refresh_if_unavailable, multi_thread test runtime, 10s HTTP timeout. Commit: a6e2278

title: Document low-priority decision gaps from memory audit
description:
## Context

The acd-2x35 memory audit identified 10 low-priority gaps — design decisions in agent memories without counterparts in `docs/decisions/`. High and medium gaps were resolved in ee5fbda.

## Gaps to document

### hook-ipc

1. **Hook subprocess polling vs wait_with_output**: `try_wait()` every 50ms chosen over `wait_with_output()` because the latter double-waits after `try_wait()`. Implementation contract for timeout enforcement. (memory line ~133)
2. **TUI hook execution is fire-and-forget**: Background thread, no result reported to TUI, output logged at debug level only. `hook-contract.md` covers Claude Code side but not TUI side. (memory line ~15)

### cli-features

3. **Restart command = force stop + start**: `acd daemon restart` reuses stop with `force=true`, then starts even if daemon wasn't running. "Restart" means "ensure running with fresh state." (memory line ~21)
4. **Uninstall preserves config file**: `acd uninstall` removes socket and hooks but deliberately keeps config, printing path for manual deletion. User data preservation choice. (memory line ~121)

### daemon-core

5. **Broadcast fires on status OR priority change**: `broadcast_session_change` triggers on either change, not just status. Distinct from original status-only design. (memory line ~53)

### tui-visual

6. **Fixed column widths**: Status=14, Priority=12, Time Elapsed=16, Session ID=40. Design choices about readability vs density. (memory line ~25)
7. **API usage widget width threshold at 30 chars**: Switches between long and compact format. Threshold value is a design choice. (memory line ~147)

### tui-rendering

8. **Scroll wheel navigates sessions, never scrolls detail panel**: Mouse scroll reserved for session list regardless of cursor position. Explicit interaction model choice. (memory line ~16)
9. **Esc and header click clear selection**: Sets `selected_index = None`. Defines what "deselect" means. (memory line ~17)

### build-tooling

10. **Pre-commit test output filtering via shell grep**: Captures cargo test output to temp file, filters dot-lines, keeps headers and summaries. Rationale: test harness only offers terse/pretty/JSON. (memory line ~42)

## Resolution

For each gap, create a new decision doc or add to an existing one. Update `docs/decisions/INDEX.md`. Some may be too minor after review — use judgment.

## Reference

Full audit report: `docs/agents/docs-api/memory-audit-report.md`
notes:
## Resolution (2026-02-26)

All 10 gaps reviewed with user:
- Gap 1: Implementation detail (Rust try_wait pitfall). Skip.
- Gap 2: → acd-fii5 (report hook results to user)
- Gap 3: Standard convention (restart = stop+start). One-line doc addition.
- Gap 4: → acd-w2gk (uninstall ask about config)
- Gap 5: Already documented in session-sorting.md.
- Gap 6: In design doc. Future: → acd-rvt7 (responsive column widths)
- Gap 7: Tuning value, track in design doc.
- Gap 8: → acd-6yux (scroll inconsistency) + acd-g09g (CWD auto-focus-back)
- Gap 9: → acd-otdg (clear focus in two-line mode)
- Gap 10: → acd-qgkw (move tooling decisions to subfolder)

Additional: acd-kkws (migrate post-merge to pre-merge-commit)
close_reason: All 10 gaps reviewed. 3 already documented, 5 implementation details, 2 became new issues. Plus 5 additional issues created from user feedback during review. See notes for full mapping.

title: 'acd daemon start' without -d blocks silently with no explanation
description:
Running `acd daemon start` without the `-d` (detach) flag starts the daemon in foreground mode, blocking the terminal indefinitely with no output or explanation.

Users may expect `daemon start` to background automatically. The foreground mode exists for debugging (see logs directly), but this intent is not communicated.

Options:
1. Print a message: "Running daemon in foreground (Ctrl+C to stop). Use -d to run in background."
2. Default to detached mode, require --foreground for debug mode
3. Just document it better in --help

Needs design discussion.

title: Raise two-line layout threshold from 5 to 7 for Terminal.app compatibility
description:
Terminal.app on macOS has a minimum window height of 6 rows. The previous TWO_LINE_LAYOUT_HEIGHT_THRESHOLD of 5 meant the two-line layout could never trigger in Terminal.app (minimum height 6 > threshold 5, so it always showed Large mode).

Changed threshold from 5 to 7 so that Terminal.app at minimum height (6 rows) correctly triggers two-line layout. Heights 7+ use Large mode.

Decision: threshold = 7 based on Terminal.app minimum of 6.

title: Improve 'acd daemon stop' warning text about active sessions
description:
Current output when stopping daemon with active sessions:

```
Warning: 6 active session(s) are running.
Stopping the daemon will disconnect the TUI but Claude Code sessions will continue.
Stop daemon anyway? (y/N): y
Daemon stopped.
```

Issues:
1. "6 active session(s) are running" is confusing — what does "active sessions" mean to the user? The daemon tracks sessions, but the warning should focus on what the user loses.
2. The warning should be about the TUI being disconnected, not about session counts.
3. Clarify what actually happens: daemon stops → TUI loses connection → sessions are still tracked by Claude Code but not visible in ACD until daemon restarts.

Needs design discussion on what the right warning message should be.

title: Review daemon SET log verbosity (session_id, status, working_dir)
description:
acd-trhx added info! logs on every SET command showing session_id, status, and working_dir. This may be too verbose for info level. Consider:

1. Is working_dir needed in every SET log line? It rarely changes.
2. Should status + working_dir be debug! and only session_id be info!?
3. Or keep as-is since SET commands are infrequent (only on hook fires)?

Decision deferred — discuss and decide.

title: Two-line layout: mouse interaction for chips and overflow indicators
description:
Add mouse support to two-line layout session chips. Three interactions:

1. Click on a chip: select that chip (set selected_index to the clicked chip).

2. Click on left overflow indicator (<- N+): scroll viewport left by 1. Focus (select) the new leftmost visible chip.

3. Click on right overflow indicator (N+ ->): scroll viewport right by 1. Focus (select) the new chip that appeared on the right (the new rightmost visible chip).

Overflow indicators are never themselves focused/selected. The selection always lands on a session chip.

Keyboard left/right arrows should have consistent behavior with mouse clicks on overflow indicators: left arrow focuses the new leftmost chip, right arrow focuses the new rightmost chip.

Mouse scroll wheel: scroll the viewport left/right without changing selection (unless selected chip scrolls out of view, then auto-scroll kicks in).

title: Session status pagination + overflow indicators
description:
Add horizontal pagination to the two-line layout session list (Line 1). Design decisions:

- Left/right arrows scroll sessions horizontally. Stop at boundaries (no wrap-around).
- Mouse scroll wheel scrolls sessions in two-line mode.
- Overflow indicators: <- N+ (left) and N+ -> (right). Non-interactive, gray when empty (no sessions in that direction).
- Selected session shown with [brackets]. Reuses app.selected_index.
- Up/down (j/k) still work in two-line mode (navigate session list).
- Sort order in two-line matches full TUI (status → priority → elapsed).
- Research (acd-4398) recommends viewport window pattern: compact_scroll_offset in App state, ~200 LOC. See docs/agents/tui-rendering/horizontal-pagination-research.md.

Agent: tui-rendering.
notes:
Ecosystem search (2026-02-18): Checked for existing horizontal pagination solutions.

1. **ratatui Tabs widget** — renders horizontal tab bar but no scroll/pagination. Truncates when tabs exceed width. No overflow indicators.

2. **tui-widgets 0.7.0 ScrollView** (formerly tui-scrollview) — generic buffer-scroll widget with horizontal scrolling support (scroll_left/scroll_right, horizontal scrollbar). Not suitable because: (a) scrolls by pixel/column, not by chip, (b) provides scrollbar indicator, not `<- N+` / `N+ ->` count indicators, (c) no auto-scroll-to-selection, (d) adds a dependency for something simpler to build custom (~200 LOC).

3. **ratatui Scrollbar widget** — visual scrollbar indicator only, no viewport management.

4. **rat-scrolled** — scroll wrapper for widgets, vertical-focused.

Conclusion: no off-the-shelf widget provides chip-level horizontal pagination with overflow count indicators. Custom viewport window approach (from acd-4398 research) is the right choice.

title: TUI: wire activate_hook + reopen_hook to double-click and keybindings
description:
## Parent

Sub-issue of acd-bwa (redesign resurrect → reopen).

## Scope

Update TUI to use the renamed/new hooks from config:

### Double-click behavior (same gesture, context-sensitive)

- Double-click on non-closed session → fire `activate_hook` (renamed from double_click_hook)
- Double-click on closed session → fire `reopen_hook` (new)
- No hook configured → show status bar message (same UX as before)

### Keybindings

- Enter on active/non-closed session → fire activate_hook
- Enter or 'r' on closed session → fire reopen_hook

### App struct changes

- Rename `double_click_hook` field → `activate_hook: Option<String>`
- Add `reopen_hook: Option<String>`
- Update `handle_mouse_event` double-click path to check session status
- Update `handle_key_event` for Enter/r

### Config field type refactor

Both hook fields must be `Option<String>` (None = no hook). This means:
- Change config schema: `activate_hook` and `reopen_hook` are `Option<String>`
  with serde default None. TOML `activate_hook = "cmd"` → Some, absent → None.
- Change App struct: `activate_hook: Option<String>` (was `double_click_hook`)
- Update main.rs config loading to pass Option directly (remove is_empty check)

### Hook execution

Same pattern as current double_click_hook:
- Substitute placeholders: {session_id}, {working_dir}, {status}
- Pipe SessionSnapshot JSON to stdin
- Fire-and-forget via sh -c

### Reopen is LOCAL only

Do NOT send IPC REOPEN to daemon. TUI just fires the hook and shows the session
as Attention locally. Broadcasting to daemon is deferred to acd-398 (P4).

### Files to change

- src/config/schema.rs (field type: String → Option<String> for both hooks)
- src/tui/app/mod.rs (App struct, handle_mouse_event, execute hooks)
- src/tui/event/ (handle_key_event for Enter/r)
- src/tui/app/tests/interaction.rs (update tests)
- src/main.rs (config loading — pass Option directly)

### Depends on

- acd-3qx (daemon reopen handler — done)
- acd-puk (config schema — done)
close_reason: activate_hook + reopen_hook wired to TUI. Context-sensitive double-click/Enter/r. 8 new tests. Commit: 2eaafa6

title: Daemon: rename resurrect → reopen + closed→active lifecycle
description:
## Parent

Sub-issue of acd-bwa (redesign resurrect → reopen).

## Scope

Rename the IPC command and daemon handler from "resurrect" to "reopen". When
daemon receives REOPEN command for a closed session:

1. Move session from closed queue → active sessions map
2. Set status to the session's last non-closed status (or Attention as default)
3. Respond with success + SessionSnapshot

### Files to change

- `src/ipc.rs`: Rename `IpcCommand::Resurrect` → `IpcCommand::Reopen`
- `src/daemon/handlers.rs` (or handlers/mod.rs): Rename handler function,
  implement closed→active transition
- `src/daemon/store/`: Add method to move session from closed to active

### Not in scope

- CLI command rename (separate issue)
- TUI keybindings (separate issue)
- Hook execution (client-side, separate issue)

### Testing

- Unit test: reopen moves session from closed to active
- Unit test: reopen on non-existent session returns error
- Unit test: reopen on already-active session returns error (or no-op)
notes:
Design clarification (2026-02-15): Reopen always sets status to Attention.
Not "last non-closed status" — Attention is semantically accurate because the
user needs to interact with the reopened session. Store method: reopen_session().

title: Sort sessions by status priority: attention > working > inactive > closed
description:
Reorder sessions in the TUI using a lexicographic sort with three keys:

1. **Status group** — attention > working > inactive > closed.
2. **User priority** (u64, descending) — higher value = ranked higher. Default: 0.
3. **Time elapsed** — within each (status, priority) group.

Implementation steps:
1. Add priority: u64 field to Session struct (default 0) in src/lib.rs
2. Add priority: u64 field to SessionSnapshot in src/ipc.rs (with serde default)
3. Include priority in From<&Session> for SessionSnapshot conversion
4. Implement sort comparator: status_group() -> priority (desc) -> elapsed
5. Add status_group() method on Status enum returning a u8 (attention=0, working=1, inactive=2, closed=3)
6. Apply sort in the TUI before rendering the session list
7. Add tests for sort ordering

Design decision (from notes): priority lives in daemon as a passively stored field. Daemon does not act on priority — stores and relays. Same pattern as working_dir.
notes:
Design decisions (session):

**Broadcast**: Rename `broadcast_status_change` → `broadcast_session_change`.
Fire on any meaningful session change (status OR priority). The internal
broadcast channel carries a lightweight signal; the SUB handler always does a
full session lookup and sends the complete SessionSnapshot to TUI.

**No new IPC command**: Priority flows through the existing SET command with
an added `--priority` flag (Option A). No separate PRIORITY command.

**Sort key**: (status_group ASC, Reverse(priority), Reverse(elapsed)).
status_group: attention=0, working=1, inactive=2, closed=3.

**CLI shape (after acd-2jp tree redesign)**: `acd session set <id> <status>
[--priority <u64>] [--working-dir <path>]`. For priority-only updates, status
becomes optional.
close_reason: Implemented: session sorting + CLI priority flag

title: Closed sessions should use same visual style as inactive sessions
description:
Closed sessions don't enter 'inactive' visual mode because is_inactive() checks !self.closed. But visually, closed sessions should look identical to inactive ones — both represent sessions the user is no longer actively using.

Fix approach (try in order):

1. **Preferred: Bind to Status enum.** Add a method like `Status::should_dim()` (or `is_dimmed()`) directly on the Status enum. This centralizes the logic — each variant knows whether it should be dimmed. Currently inactive and closed should return true; working, attention, question should return false. This is the most idiomatic Rust pattern.

2. **Alternative: Rename is_inactive().** If approach 1 doesn't fit the current architecture, rename `is_inactive()` to `should_dim()` (or `should_have_dim_style()`) and include closed sessions in the check. Do NOT keep the name `is_inactive` if it now covers closed sessions too — the name must match the semantics.

3. **Fallback: Separate method.** If `is_inactive()` is used for non-visual logic elsewhere (e.g., daemon idle timer), keep it unchanged and add a new `should_dim()` method that returns true for both inactive and closed.

Check all callers of `is_inactive()` to understand whether it's purely visual or used for other logic too. This determines which approach to use.

Visual requirements:
- Closed sessions get the same dimmed style as inactive sessions (identical, no subtle difference)
- Closed + highlighted sessions get the same Color::Black text treatment as inactive + highlighted (from acd-87o)

Related: acd-87o (highlighted inactive text readability) — merged.
close_reason: Closed

title: Limit TUI to 1 frame per second
description:
Problem: without throttling, the TUI recalculates elapsed time (diff between last history timestamp and now, then format) for every session on every frame. At scale (100 sessions × 100 TUIs = 10,000 calculations per tick), this drains CPU for no user-visible benefit.

Solution: two rendering modes with different trigger rates.

1. PASSIVE ticks (elapsed time recalculation): limit to a constant interval (default 1 second). Define a const like ELAPSED_TIME_REFRESH_INTERVAL (Duration). Zero-cost abstraction, no overhead.

2. USER INPUT (keyboard, mouse, scroll): render immediately on each input event. Cursor movement (j/k/arrows), scrolling, clicking must be real-time with no lag. If user presses down 5 times quickly, the cursor must track all 5 moves immediately.

This is NOT a global fps cap. Input events bypass the throttle entirely. Only the background elapsed-time tick is throttled.

The agent must understand the current event loop and tick rate before changing it. Discover how the TUI currently processes events and renders frames.

title: Resolve cargo audit warnings from ratatui dependencies
description:
CI cargo audit reports 2 warnings, both from ratatui 0.29.0 transitive dependencies:

## Advisories

### RUSTSEC-2024-0436: paste (unmaintained)
- Crate: paste v1.0.15
- Severity: unmaintained (no longer maintained)
- Chain: paste 1.0.15 ← ratatui 0.29.0 ← agent-console-dashboard

### RUSTSEC-2026-0002: lru (unsound)
- Crate: lru v0.12.5
- Title: IterMut violates Stacked Borrows by invalidating internal pointer
- Severity: unsound (memory safety issue)
- Chain: lru 0.12.5 ← ratatui 0.29.0 ← agent-console-dashboard

## Resolution paths

1. **Upgrade ratatui to 0.30.0** — check if the new version bumps paste/lru to fixed versions
2. **Cargo patch** — override transitive deps in Cargo.toml if ratatui 0.30.0 doesn't fix it
3. **Upstream PR** — contribute fix to ratatui if they haven't addressed it
4. **Allowlist** — last resort: add to audit deny.toml with justification (currently these are allowed warnings in CI)

## Current CI behavior
Audit job passes because these are warnings, not errors. But unsound memory in lru is a real concern.
close_reason: All security vulns already patched. Two INFO/unsoundness warnings (paste 1.0.15 unmaintained, lru 0.12.5 unsound) are transitive deps from ratatui 0.29 — requires ratatui 0.30 upgrade (breaking API). Not actionable without major version bump.

title: Plugin distribution strategy decision (B1+B4)
description:
## Problem

Two hook installation paths exist with no clear strategy for which to use when:

| Path | Method | Audience | Status |
|------|--------|----------|--------|
| acd install | Writes to ~/.claude/settings.json | Personal use | Working |
| .claude-plugin/plugin.json | Build artifact from build.rs | Distribution | Unclear |

### Issues with the Plugin Path

1. .claude-plugin/ is gitignored — won't ship via git clone
2. Plugin marketplace discovery is undocumented — unclear how to publish
3. claude plugin install requires a marketplace entry (local or remote)
4. build.rs generates plugin.json but nobody can use it without the binary

### Issues with settings.json Path

1. Works perfectly for personal use
2. Cannot distribute to others (requires them to have acd installed first)
3. Hardcodes binary path — won't work if acd is at a different location

### Decision Needed

Choose one of:

A. **settings.json only**: Remove plugin system from build.rs. acd install is the
   only installation method. Distribution via cargo install + acd install.
   Plugin marketplace not needed.

B. **Both paths**: Keep plugin.json for future marketplace publishing. Use
   acd install for now. Revisit plugin distribution when Claude Code marketplace
   is more mature.

C. **Plugin-first**: Research Claude Code plugin marketplace requirements. Publish
   plugin. Make acd install a fallback for users who prefer manual setup.

### Recommendation

Option B (keep both, defer marketplace). The plugin infrastructure costs nothing
to maintain (build.rs generates it automatically). When the marketplace matures,
we can publish without rework.

## References

- Blockers doc: B1 and B4
- Claude Code plugins reference: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference

title: Track mf-upm
Redesign resurrect → reopen: fire configurable hook instead of printing command
description:
## Current behavior (problematic)

`acd resurrect <id>` looks up a closed session, validates the working directory,
and returns `claude --resume <id>` as text. User has to copy-paste it. The TUI
has a TODO (Action::Resurrect) that doesn't send the IPC command yet.

## New design direction

Rename "resurrect" to "reopen" throughout the codebase. Instead of returning a
command string, fire a configurable hook from the ACD config file.

### Two hooks in config

1. **activate hook** (`tui.activate_hook`): Focus on an active session — e.g.,
   switch terminal pane to it. Fires when user activates a non-closed session.
2. **reopen hook** (`tui.reopen_hook`): Reopen a closed session — e.g., run
   `claude --resume` in the right directory. Fires only for closed sessions.

Both hooks receive the full SessionSnapshot as JSON on stdin (same pattern as
double-click hook). Placeholder substitution: `{session_id}`, `{working_dir}`,
`{status}`.

### Implementation scope

- Rename `resurrect` → `reopen` in CLI, IPC command, daemon handler, TUI action
- Replace text output with hook execution
- Add `tui.activate_hook` and `tui.reopen_hook` to config schema
- TUI: 'r' key or Enter on closed session fires reopen hook
- TUI: Enter on active session fires activate hook
- CLI: `acd session reopen <id>` fires the reopen hook via daemon

### Open questions

- Should the daemon remove the session from the closed queue after reopen?
  (Currently resurrect does this — may want to keep it until a new SessionStart
  hook arrives for the resumed session)
- How does this interact with multiplexer integration (acd-7u2)?
notes:
Split into 6 sequential sub-issues (2026-02-15):

1. acd-3qx — Daemon: rename resurrect → reopen + closed→active (daemon-core)
2. acd-puk — Config: add activate_hook + reopen_hook (cli-features)
3. acd-1j2 — TUI: wire hooks to double-click + keybindings (tui-rendering)
4. acd-jau — CLI: rename resurrect → session reopen (cli-features)
5. acd-0hx — Delete integrations/zellij module (cleanup)
6. acd-3le — Delete terminal/ module (cleanup)

Execution: sequential, one agent at a time, merge after each.
Double-click becomes context-sensitive: non-closed → activate_hook, closed → reopen_hook.
Reopen moves session from closed → active queue in daemon.
Multiplexer interaction: just hook configuration, no special code.
close_reason: All 6 sub-issues closed (3qx, puk, jau, 0hx, 3le, 1j2). Resurrect→reopen migration complete.

title: Add uninstall command for agent-console-dashboard system
description:
## Scope

Enhance `acd uninstall` to clean up the full ACD system.

### Already done (acd-qe8)
- Remove hooks from Claude Code settings.json

### Remaining
1. **Socket cleanup**: Remove `/tmp/agent-console-dashboard.sock` if it exists
2. **Config file**: Do NOT remove. Print a one-liner command the user can copy-paste to remove it themselves: `rm <config_path>`
3. **Stop daemon**: If running, stop it before cleanup

### NOT in scope
- launchd/systemd service files — ACD has no system service (lazy-start design)

```
  Stopped daemon
  Removed socket file
  Config file preserved at: ~/.config/agent-console-dashboard/config.toml
  To remove: rm ~/.config/agent-console-dashboard/config.toml
```

Hooks uninstall done (acd-qe8). Remaining: stop daemon, remove socket, print config path.

## Investigation Complete (2026-02-09)

### Original conclusion was WRONG
elicitation_dialog is for MCP tool elicitation, NOT AskUserQuestion.

### Solution: PreToolUse with matcher AskUserQuestion
Confirmed by Anthropic collaborator @dicksontsai in #10168.

2. PermissionRequest matcher AskUserQuestion — also fires, duplicates with #1
3. Stop hook — too broad (every response, not AskUserQuestion-specific)
4. iTerm2 triggers — terminal-specific, fragile
5. CLAUDE.md prompt engineering — non-deterministic

### Related GitHub issues
- #13024 (OPEN) — Main feature request for WaitingForInput hook
- #10168 (OPEN) — Where Anthropic staff posted the workaround
- #12048 (CLOSED dup of #10168)
- #13830 (CLOSED dup of #13024)
- #15872 (OPEN) — Another request for AskUserQuestion hook
- #12605 (CLOSED dup of #10168) — Bidirectional hook request
- #13439 (CLOSED fixed v2.0.76) — Bug that broke PreToolUse+AskUserQuestion

### Action required for ACD
1. Replace Notification/elicitation_dialog hook with PreToolUse/AskUserQuestion hook
2. Or add PreToolUse/AskUserQuestion as a 7th hook alongside existing elicitation_dialog
3. Test that question status fires correctly in TUI
4. Verify no blank-answer regression on current Claude Code version

title: Investigate usage fetch timer alignment and rounding
description: Investigation complete. Timer uses tokio::interval(180s), no drift. Config usage_fetch_interval defined but NOT wired to daemon. D4 alignment concern deferred. Remaining work: (1) wire config to daemon, (2) investigate if rounding causes real issues, (3) consider API window boundary alignment.
notes:
## Investigation Results (2026-02-09)

### Timer: No drift
tokio::interval(180s) handles drift correctly. No issue here.

### Config: usage_fetch_interval not wired
The config field exists but is NOT connected to the daemon timer. Needs wiring.

### Rounding Solution (confirmed by user)
5-hour API window = 300 minutes. Fetch every 3 minutes = 1% of window.

**Solution: round down (floor) the displayed time percentage.**

Why floor:
1. **Truthful** — never overstates elapsed time. 50.7% real → shows 50%.
2. **Clean 1% steps** — each fetch advances real value by ~1%. Floor produces exactly +1% per fetch (50.2% → 50%, next fetch 51.2% → 51%).
3. **Alignment tolerant** — first fetch may not land on window boundary. Floor absorbs: 0.8% → shows 0%, next → 1%. No jitter.

Round-to-nearest would also give 1% steps but overstates by up to 0.5%, creating misleading rate signals when comparing time vs quota.

### Remaining Work
1. Wire usage_fetch_interval config to daemon timer
2. Implement floor rounding for displayed time percentage
close_reason: Split into two separate issues: acd-6hr (wire config to daemon) and acd-edx (floor rounding)

handles it locally (sets status to Attention in UI). Should the TUI also send
an IPC REOPEN command to the daemon so the daemon knows the session is active?

Pros of broadcasting: daemon state stays accurate, other TUI consumers see
the change.

Cons: TUI currently only receives from daemon (subscription), never sends.
Adding send capability increases complexity significantly.

Deferred from acd-1j2 to avoid scope creep.

title: Add interactive TUI E2E tests with terminal emulation
description:
Add automated tests that exercise the TUI's interactive event loop — key presses,
mouse clicks, timing — not just rendered output.

## Context

Current TUI tests use ratatui's TestBackend to render frames and assert on cell
content, colors, and layout. These cover rendering correctness but not the
interactive loop (event handling, state transitions from real input sequences).

The e2e-test-plan.md notes "TUI (step 7) cannot be automated and must be tested
manually." This issue aims to change that.

## Approaches to Evaluate

### PTY-based crates (recommended starting point)

- **expectrl** (https://docs.rs/expectrl): Spawn a PTY, send keystrokes, match
  output patterns. Rust-native, no external deps.
- **rexpect** (https://docs.rs/rexpect): Similar expect-style PTY automation.

These would launch `acd tui` in a real terminal emulator and verify output.
Added as dev-dependencies only — no production impact.

### Snapshot testing with insta

- **insta** (https://docs.rs/insta): Snapshot/golden-file testing. Combined with
  TestBackend, captures full terminal buffer as text for regression detection.
- Lower effort than PTY tests, catches unintended layout changes.

### Custom event injection harness (higher effort)

- Make App::run() accept an event source trait
- Inject synthetic crossterm::event::Event values in tests
- No external tool deps, fast and deterministic
- Moderate refactor — consider only if PTY crates prove insufficient

### VHS (visual demos, not assertions)

- charmbracelet/vhs: Records terminal sessions from scripts
- Good for visual regression screenshots, not programmatic assertions

## Test Scenarios to Cover

- j/k navigation moves highlight marker
- Enter opens detail panel, Esc closes it
- Mouse click selects session, double-click fires hook
- q exits cleanly
- Scroll wheel navigates list and detail history
- Resize triggers responsive layout change
- Header click clears selection

## Notes

- crossterm provides the Event enum (key, mouse, resize) that drives the loop
- ratatui's TestBackend already used extensively in existing unit tests
- PTY crates are dev-dependencies only, acceptable for test infrastructure
- Discuss approach in detail when working on this issue

title: Multiplexer integration (Zellij layouts and launcher)
description:
Integrate ACD dashboard as a top pane in Zellij terminal multiplexer layouts. Three layout presets: minimal (2-row), default (3-row), detailed (5-row). Includes launcher script, KDL layout files, and installation to ~/.config/zellij/layouts/. Scope note: this is about the broader 'agent console' concept — the dashboard as one component in a multiplexer workspace with work panes below. See design/integrations.md for the multiplexer integration philosophy.

---

## Original Spec (from docs/guides/zellij.md)

### Prerequisites

- **Zellij installed** (version 0.39.0+ recommended)
  - Installation guide: https://zellij.dev/documentation/installation
- **agent-console binary in PATH**
  - Build: cargo build --release
  - Install: cargo install --path .

### Quick Start

Use the provided launcher script to start Zellij with the dashboard:

```sh
# Default layout (2-line dashboard + vertical split)
./scripts/zellij/zellij-claude.sh

# Minimal layout (1-line dashboard)
./scripts/zellij/zellij-claude.sh minimal

# Detailed layout (multi-line dashboard)
./scripts/zellij/zellij-claude.sh detailed
```

The script automatically:
- Checks for Zellij installation
- Validates Zellij version (warns if < 0.39.0)
- Prevents nested sessions
- Launches the appropriate layout

### Layout Descriptions

Three pre-configured layouts are available:

| Layout | Pane Height | Content | Use Case |
|--------|------------|---------|----------|
| minimal | 2 rows | Session names + status | Maximum screen space for work |
| default | 3 rows | Status + working directory | Balanced monitoring and space |
| detailed | 5 rows | Full session details | Deep session inspection |

#### Minimal Layout

```kdl
layout {
    pane size=2 {
        command "agent-console"
        args "tui" "--layout" "one-line"
    }
    pane
}
```

#### Default Layout

```kdl
layout {
    pane size=3 {
        command "agent-console"
        args "tui" "--layout" "two-line"
    }
    pane split_direction="vertical" {
        pane
        pane
    }
}
```

#### Detailed Layout

```kdl
layout {
    pane size=5 {
        command "agent-console"
        args "tui"
    }
    pane
}
```

### Installation to Zellij Config

```sh
mkdir -p ~/.config/zellij/layouts
cp scripts/zellij/layouts/claude-*.kdl ~/.config/zellij/layouts/
zellij --layout claude-default
```

### Usage Patterns

#### Launching from Outside Zellij

```sh
./scripts/zellij/zellij-claude.sh default
zellij --layout claude-default
```

#### Adding Dashboard to Existing Session

```sh
zellij action new-pane --command agent-console tui
```

### Daemon Auto-Start

The dashboard automatically triggers daemon auto-start when launched. No manual daemon management is required.

### Troubleshooting

- Zellij not installed → brew install zellij / cargo install zellij
- Nested session warning → exit current session or use zellij action new-pane
- Version compatibility → update to 0.39.0+
- Layout file not found → run from project root
- agent-console not found → cargo install --path .
- No data shown → check daemon status, ensure active sessions exist

### Customization

Custom layouts: copy existing KDL, modify pane size and args. Save to ~/.config/zellij/layouts/custom.kdl.

Dashboard placement: reverse pane order in KDL to place at bottom instead of top.

### Platform Support

Tested on macOS (Sonoma 14.x+) and Linux (Ubuntu 22.04+, Arch, Fedora). Windows via WSL.

### References

- Zellij docs: https://zellij.dev/documentation/
- KDL layout syntax: https://zellij.dev/documentation/creating-a-layout.html

title: Consider actor model refactor for daemon (deferred)
description: Amendment 2 in decision-amendments.md proposed replacing tokio::spawn-per-connection + Arc<RwLock<HashMap>> with single-threaded actor model + plain HashMap. After analysis, decided to defer: Rust type system already prevents data races, TOCTOU risk is marginal at <50 connections, and the refactor touches ~1600 lines across server.rs/store.rs/usage.rs. Revisit if: (1) actual concurrency bugs appear, (2) connection count grows significantly, or (3) lock contention shows up in profiling. See discussion in devlog.
